comment.body:
so the yellow line is the rolling mean on a volume weighted basis.
this gives you an idea where the momentum starts to be stretched and the porbability for mean reversion is going up (i plotted 1.5 and 2.5 sigma lvls to provide probability for you)

however, as I wrote in my education post about mean reversion, it doesn't mean that the price retests its mean, but its averages itslef down to its mean value, so it will always overshoot the mean.
the margin of error area is based on my own experience, it gives you the area that is healty for the price to stay in, if it breaks the rolling mean. but once it closes out of this area, the directional move is pretty much confirmed.

comment.body:
lets call "A" the stocks with positive returns, and "B" the stocks with negative returns.
calculating (A-B)/(A+B) will give you a number in the [-1; 1] range.
if the ratio is negative, it means that negative returns start to outweight the positive ones. 

market with higher breadth means that the movement has momentum and it is less prone to sudden collapse.
but if markets showing divergence between this ratio vs the returns, then it foreshadows a reversion.

comment.body:
Listen..
vanna flips are the strongest daily borderlines. they literally depends on vol, and only on vol.
but you must know that intraday, the hedgingflow is determined mostly by gamma. thats the main driver.
btu when gamma is low, thats where vanna impact comes.
and speed profile usually dictates the vol regime.

i tell you why:
if you have a positive speed, it means spot up/gamma up. 
but it also means, that spot up/vol down, bcs gamma works inversely to vol.
logical, bcs when mm is longer gamma, he has to sell more against the price action, that naturally slows down the momentum, compresses the range of returns, and it causes vol to come down. Other way to think about it: if gamma is higher on a contract, then its IV will be lower.  so if the positioning is longer gamma up, then it is also lower IV. 

if speed is negative, then spot up/gamma down: so if market moves up, less gamma, MMs have to sell less and less against the price (or even buy into the momentum, if they flip negative gamma), so they let vol to expand or even help it to expand.
So in a short speed environment, vol naturally goes up if spot goes up.

comment.body:
Now... think about it: if you have a zero vanna flip upwards.
If MMs are long speed, then they are net long vanna, so above the vanna flip they flip to negative vanna.
As said before, if spot moves up in a long speed environment, vol comes down. Long vanna = vol down/delta down, triggers more buying, supports the upwards momenutm. But above the flip, it shifts to short vanna, where vol down/delta up... so MMs exposure becomes longer delta, that leads to sudden sell.
This is why it can be broken only, if vol starts to move up against gamma.
And that behavior is always a result of an anomaly, some sudden increased customer interest of buying vol upthere, and zomma then dumps gammas.

comment.body:
Same happens, if we are in short speed environemtn, and we have a vanna flip above us. 
Vol goes up, as spot moves higher. In negative speed vanna is also net negative, so vol up/delta down, forcing MMs to buy more futs supporting the price. 
But when the spot reaches the flip, vanna goes from short to long, where high vol pushes deltas up, so MMs switch into selling mode. 
This is why you have to see vol down against the decreasing gamma in a short speed environment to arrive healty into a long vanna environment. And this coud be a result of cusotmer vol selling interest, naturally increasing gammas through zomma effect.
